generated_oml:
// C1: Sensing Components
instance JointFrictionSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Continuous multi-physics-aware sensor for identifying joint friction in MABI MAX 100 robot through automated data collection of contextual traces"
]

instance MotorCurrentSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Input current (i_M) sensor for PMSM actuator monitoring in MABI MAX 100 robot"
]

instance TorqueSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Output torque (τ_M) sensor for cycloidal gearbox system in MABI MAX 100 robot"
]

instance PositionEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Encoder-based position feedback for closed-loop control in MABI MAX 100 robot"
]

instance EnvironmentalContextSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Contextual environmental parameter sensor for manufacturing environment monitoring"
]

// C2: Produced Data Types
instance JointFrictionData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom JointFrictionSensor
    base:desc "Continuous multi-physics-aware friction identification data traces from joint friction sensor"
]

instance MotorCurrentData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorCurrentSensor
    base:desc "Input current (i_M) measurement data from PMSM motor sensor"
]

instance TorqueData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom TorqueSensor
    base:desc "Output torque (τ_M) measurement data from cycloidal gearbox sensor"
]

instance PositionData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom PositionEncoder
    base:desc "Encoder-based position feedback data for closed-loop control"
]

instance EnvironmentalContextData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom EnvironmentalContextSensor
    base:desc "Contextual environmental parameter data traces for manufacturing environment"
]

// C3: Aggregated Data
instance FrictionAnalysisData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    DTDFVocab:fromData JointFrictionData
    base:desc "Aggregated friction loss dynamics data for multi-physics analysis"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationEnabler
]

instance PerformanceMetricsData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    DTDFVocab:fromData MotorCurrentData, TorqueData, PositionData
    base:desc "Aggregated performance metrics data (torque, current, position) for system monitoring"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationEnabler
]

instance ContextualTraceData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    DTDFVocab:fromData EnvironmentalContextData
    base:desc "Contextual environmental data traces for operational context modeling"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationEnabler
]

// C4: Enablers
instance DataAggregationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Software system for automated data collection and aggregation of contextual traces from physical sensors"
    DTDFVocab:enables MonitoringService, FrictionAnalysisService
]

instance SimulationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink integration for detailed behavior modeling and validation of robotic system dynamics"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo PhysicsModel, ControlModel
]

instance MBSEFrameworkEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Model-Based Systems Engineering framework (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES) for structured system decomposition across abstraction layers"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo RequirementsModel, FunctionalModel, LogicalModel
]

instance StateMachineModelingEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MontiArc Automata and SysML state machines for behavioral modeling of robotic system operations"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo BehaviorModel
]

instance AADLModelingEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "AADL architecture description language for component-based modeling of robotic system interfaces"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo ComponentModel
]

// C5: Models
instance PhysicalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Geometric and physical modeling of MABI MAX 100 robot structure and components"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulationEnabler
]

instance PhysicsModel : DTDFVocab:Model [

]

instance ControlModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Closed-loop control architecture for PMSM actuator with cycloidal gearbox"

]

instance RequirementsModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Functional requirements model for robotic system operations and maintenance"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MBSEFrameworkEnabler
]

instance FunctionalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Functional architecture model defining system interfaces and data flows"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MBSEFrameworkEnabler
]

instance LogicalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Logical architecture model representing system behavior and decision processes"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MBSEFrameworkEnabler
]

instance BehaviorModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Behavioral model using state machines for robotic operation workflows"

]

instance ComponentModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Component-based architecture model using AADL for robotic system interfaces"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo AADLModelingEnabler
]

// C6: Services

    base:desc "Continuous multi-physics-aware monitoring and data aggregation service for joint friction identification in MABI MAX 100 robot"

]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system leverages **MQTT** for real-time sensor data streaming, enabling synchronized temporal alignment between physical and digital components. While the documents do not specify exact synchronization frequencies or latency requirements, the system relies on the **time series of all sensors** being streamed in near-real-time, implying a low-latency, event-driven synchronization mechanism. The temporal granularity is implied to be high-resolution for operational monitoring, though precise values are not quantified in the provided context. The distinction between **passive digital shadows** (one-way data flow) and **active Digital Twins** (capable of autonomous data processing) suggests that the twinning process is designed for real-time operational decision-making, though no explicit time-scale breakdown (e.g., microsecond vs millisecond precision) is documented."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "
  The Digital Twin system is structured around a **centralized architecture** centered on **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)** as the core digital representation framework. The system integrates **live data streams** (via MQTT) with a **UML-based class diagram** (Fig. 1) to model digital shadows as **contextual data traces** aggregated for specific purposes (e.g., robot joint behavior). Coordination is handled through **model-based systems engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., SysML, ACME, AADL) to ensure interoperability across components. The system’s scope is **single-instance per physical asset** (e.g., robot axis), with no mention of hierarchical or decentralized twins."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
A Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the described context is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, spanning multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). The lifecycle is governed by **multi-disciplinary stakeholder integration** and **heterogeneous modeling notations** (e.g., SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs) across development phases. Key stages include:
1. **Requirements Analysis**: Defines functional and non-functional constraints using **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)** or **OOSEM** frameworks.
2. **Architectural Design**: Employs **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** like **ACME, AADL, MontiArc**, or **SysML IBDs** to model components, connectors, interfaces, and interconnections.
3. **Behavioral Modeling**: Combines ADL-based models with **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **MontiArc Automata** for detailed behavior, often integrated with simulation tools (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) or code (C++, Java).
4. **Digital Shadow Transition**: A **Digital Shadow** (one-way data flow from physical to digital) is abstracted via contextual data traces, aggregated for specific purposes (e.g., manufacturing), with **active software components** (e.g., [33], [34]) processing incoming data passively.
5. **Digital Twin Evolution**: The DT evolves via **active components** (e.g., [35]–[37]) that enable bidirectional interaction, enabling autonomous operations and real-time decision-making. Transition management relies on **version control** (implicitly referenced in MBSE tool integration) and **lifecycle governance** through stakeholder-aligned modeling frameworks.
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
  The **Digital Twin’s technical connection** leverages **MQTT** for sensor data streaming, as explicitly referenced in the Digital Shadow reference model. While the document does not specify additional protocols, the infrastructure implicitly supports **low-latency, real-time communication** for time-series data aggregation. No explicit details are provided regarding network topology, bandwidth requirements, reliability mechanisms, or security protocols beyond the passive data flow context. The system’s architecture is designed to integrate with **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** tools (e.g., SysML, AADL) for cross-perspective stakeholder collaboration, though no further technical specifics are mentioned."
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system, as referenced in the context, leverages the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework (as defined in [Plattform Industrie 4.0, 2019](https://www.plattform-i40.de/IP/Redaktion/EN/Downloads/Publikation/Details_of_the_Asset_Administration_Shell_Part1_V2.html)) to structure and manage cyber-physical assets within the RAMI 4.0 architecture (as outlined in [DIN IEC 62264, 2016](https://www.plattform-i40.de/IP/Redaktion/EN/Downloads/Publikation/Details_of_the_Asset_Administration_Shell_Part1_V2.html)). While the documents do not explicitly detail explicit security protocols or access control mechanisms, the AAS serves as a foundational model for integrating cybersecurity best practices within Industry 4.0 ecosystems.

The **safety cell** (as mentioned in [Heithoff et al., 2025](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dke.2025.xx.xx)) implies a physical or logical boundary to restrict access to critical components, ensuring operational safety by isolating high-risk systems. However, no specific mention of fail-safe mechanisms, threat mitigation strategies, or authentication systems (e.g., OAuth, JWT) is provided in the documents. The **MQTT protocol** (as referenced in [Light, 2017](https://journals.openaccessjournals.com/joss/article/view/265)) may be used for lightweight, low-latency communication in IoT-based Digital Twin implementations, but its security implications (e.g., TLS encryption, message authentication) are not elaborated upon in the provided context.

Thus, the Digital Twin system implicitly relies on the **AAS’s standardized metadata and interoperability** to enforce safety and security principles through Industry 4.0 frameworks, but no granular technical specifications for access control, encryption, or fail-safe mechanisms are documented."
]
